attributes.stem: A 55-year-old woman presents for review of her blood pressure. Today it is 160/100mmHg, which has not improved since her last review three months ago. She has been taking perindopril 5mg daily, and reports that she is compliant. She is a non-smoker and has been exercising three times a week since she was counselled on appropriate lifestyle modifications. She has had an intermittent dry cough over the past three months, but this does not bother her greatly. She has normal renal function.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
According to the current Australian guidelines for hypertension management (2016), blood pressure targets should be individualised through shared decision-making between patient and clinician, taking into account the potential benefits and harms of treatment, and reviewed regularly. In general, a target blood pressure of <140/90 mmHg is recommended for patients with uncomplicated hypertension. In patients at high of cardiovascular risk—such as those with previous myocardial infarction or stroke, diabetes with microalbuminuria, moderate to severe CKD or familial hypercholesterolaemia—more intensive systolic blood pressure targets (<120 mmHg) may be appropriate with close patient monitoring.

In this case aiming for a blood pressure target of <140/90 mmHg is appropriate. This patient’s hypertension has not responded to monotherapy with an ACE inhibitor. This is not unusual; monotherapy is ineffective in achieving this target in 70% of patients.  She has had normal blood tests (no changes in renal function), can tolerate the side effects (cough), and is compliant with the medication. The next most appropriate step is to add another agent rather than increase the dose of the ACE inhibitor to reduce the risk of potentiating the side effects of perindopril. Either a thiazide diuretic or dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker would be suitable - both are proven to reduce cardiovascular events.

Verapamil, a non-dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker (CCB),  shows significant cardiac effects in contrast to amlodipine, a dihydropyridine CCB, due to its action on vascular smooth muscle and cardiac myocytes. This results in negative inotropic and chronotropic effects, leading to symptomatic bradycardia. On the other hand, amlodipine primarily affects vascular smooth muscle, causing vasodilation and a reduction in systemic vascular resistance with minimal cardiac depressive effects, which makes it more suitable for hypertension control and less likely to cause symptomatic bradycardia. The cardiac depressive properties of non-dihydropyridine CCBs like Verapamil, including the risk of exacerbating heart failure, make them less favourable for managing hypertension compared to dihydropyridine CCBs.

Beta-blockers such as atenolol have been shown to be inferior to calcium channel blockers in reducing the incidence of stroke and all-cause mortality. Beta-blockers are therefore considered second line unless there is a compelling indication such as heart failure. If, after a further three months, this patient’s target has not been reached and both drugs have been well tolerated, one dose (excluding thiazide diuretics) may be increased incrementally to the maximum recommended dose.

Furosemide is a loop diuretic and is not a first-line agent for hypertension.
learningPoints: In a patient where monotherapy not achieved the target range of blood pressure control, it is more effective to introduce a second class of drug, rather than increasing the dose of the first agent.
references: National Heart Foundation of Australia. Guideline for the diagnosis and management of hypertension in adults – 2016 [Internet]. Melbourne: National Heart Foundation of Australia; 2016 [cited 2025 May 23]. Available from: https://assets.contentstack.io/v3/assets/blt8a393bb3b76c0ede/bltbf3d36e10b48f01f/65b0963ea933e532ae0286de/01_Hypertension-guideline-2016_WEB.pdf
Guerrero-Garcia C, Rubio-Guerra A. Combination therapy in the treatment of hypertension. Drugs in Context. 2018;7:212531.

Hutton B, Yazdi F, Hersi M et al. Drug Safety and Effectiveness Network Report: A Systematic Review with Network Meta-Analyses and Economic Evaluation Comparing Therapies for Hypertension in Non-Diabetic Patients. Ottawa: University of Ottawa 2015.
